coredata.dc:description: Intelligent onsite accurate monitoring ethyl carbamate (EC, a group 2 A carcinogen) in environment is of great significance to safeguard environmental health and public safety. Herein, we reported an intelligent dual-modal point-of-care (POC) assay based on the bimetallic Mn and Ce co-doped oxidase-like fluorescence carbon dots (Ce&MnCDs) nanozyme-driven competitive effect. In brief, the oxidase-like activity of Ce&MnCDs was inhibited by thiocholine (TCh, originating from the hydrolysis of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) to acetylthiocholine (ATCh)), preventing the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP). However, with the aid of Br2 + NaOH, EC inactivated AChE to prevent TCh generation for re-launching the oxidase-like activity of Ce&MnCDs to trigger the oxidation of OPD into DAP, thereby outputting an EC concentration-dependent ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric readouts by employing Ce&MnCDs and OPD as the optical signal reporters. Interestingly, these dual-modal optical signals could be transduced into the gray values that was linearly proportional to the residual levels of EC on a smartphone-based portable platform, with a detection limit down to 1.66 μg/mL, qualifying the requirements of analysis of EC residues in real samples. This opened up a new avenue for onsite assessment of the risk of residues of EC, safeguarding environmental health and public safety.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The process of synthesizing Ce&Mn@CDs. (B) Schematic diagram of the intelligent onsite dual-modal detection of EC based on the oxidase-like fluorescent Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme-driven competition effect.

(A) TEM images of Ce&Mn@CDs. (B) STEM image, and (C, D, E, F, G) elemental mapping of C, N, O, Mn andCe, respectively. (H) XPS spectrum of CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs. (I) XPS spectrum of the C 1 s electrons of CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs. (J) XPS spectrum of the Mn 2p electrons of Ce&Mn@CDs. (K) XPS spectrum of the Ce 3d electrons of Ce&Mn@CDs. (L) Hydrodynamic diameter of CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs in DI water. (M) Normalized fluorescence spectra of Ce&Mn@CDs under different excitations. Inset: (left) Naked eye photo of Ce&Mn@CDs, (right) Fluorescence image of 365 nm UV lamp excitation in D.I. water.

(A) Absorption spectra of (1) Ce&Mn@CDs + TMB, (2) CDs, (3) Ce&Mn@CDs, (4) TMB, and (5) CDs + TMB in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0) for 15.0 min incubation at 40.0 ◦C, respectively. (B) Comparison of absorbance at 650 nm for the absorption spectra shown in Fig. 3 A. Inset: the corresponding photos from left to right. (C) EPR spectrum of DMPO and DMPO + Ce&Mn@CDs. (D) EPR spectrum of TEMP and TEMP + Ce&Mn@CDs. Absorption spectra of TMB catalyzed by Ce&Mn@CDs at different (E) pH and (F) temperatures. Experimental conditions (E-F): Ce&Mn@CDs, 50.0 μg/mL; TMB, 1.0 mM; HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0); 15.0 min; 40.0 °C.

(A) Absorption at 440 nm and (B) fluorescence intensity ratios F550 nm/F446 nm of (1) Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD, (2) Br2 + NaOH, (3) Ce&Mn@CDs, (4) OPD, (5) Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE, (6) Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE + EC, (7) Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE + Br2 + NaOH, and (8) Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE + Br2 + NaOH + EC systems. Inset: the corresponding naked eye and fluorescence images from left to right. (C) Fluorescence and (E) absorption spectra of the Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE system after incubation with different concentrations of 0.0 – 400.0 μg/L of EC pre-treated with Br2 + NaOH. (D) Fluorescence intensity ratio F 550 nm/F 446 nm and (F) absorbance at 440 nm for Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE system after incubation with different concentrations of 0.0 – 400.0 μg/L of EC pre-treated with Br2 + NaOH. Inset: the corresponding EC concentration-dependent fluorescence and naked eye images from left to right. Experimental conditions: Ce&Mn@CDs, 50.0 μg/mL; OPD, 10.0 mM; ATChC, 10.0 mM; AChE, 20.0 mU/mL; HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0); 40.0 °C.

(A) Schematic diagram of the dual-modal detection process of EC in actual alcoholic beverage matrices. (B & C) The calibration curves of the G/B values in fluorescence and colorimetric modes versus EC concentrations as shown in Fig. 5 A. (D & E) Selectivity analysis of the Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE system for EC detection by comparing the G/B values in fluorescence and colorimetric modes. 1–12 were EC, urea, lactic acid, ethyl lactate, isoamyl alcohol, arginine, glutamic acid, glycine, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ , and blanks, respectively. Inset: the corresponding photographs. (F & G) Anti-interference analysis of the Ce&Mn@CDs + OPD + ATChC + AChE system for EC detection by comparing the G/B values in fluorescence and colorimetric modes. 1–11 were blank, urea, lactic acid, arginine, glutamic acid, ethyl lactate, isoamyl alcohol, glycine, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+, respectively. Experimental conditions: Ce&Mn@CDs, 50.0 μg/mL; OPD, 10.0 mM; ATChC, 10.0 mM; AChE, 20.0 mU/mL; HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0); 40.0 °C. All of the involved EC here was pretreated with 25.0 μL of the mixture of Br2 + NaOH at the volume ratio of V:V = 5:1 for 15.0 min.

Smartphone-assisted Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme drive competitive effects of smart onsite ratiometric fluorescence (F) and colorimetric (C) synergistic dual-modal analytical platforms for the detection of EC in real samples.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intelligent onsite accurate monitoring ethyl carbamate (EC, a group 2 A carcinogen) in environment is of great significance to safeguard environmental health and public safety. Herein, we reported an intelligent dual-modal point-of-care (POC) assay based on the bimetallic Mn and Ce co-doped oxidase-like fluorescence carbon dots (Ce&MnCDs) nanozyme-driven competitive effect. In brief, the oxidase-like activity of Ce&MnCDs was inhibited by thiocholine (TCh, originating from the hydrolysis of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) to acetylthiocholine (ATCh)), preventing the oxidation of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) to 2,3-diaminophenothiazine (DAP). However, with the aid of Br2 + NaOH, EC inactivated AChE to prevent TCh generation for re-launching the oxidase-like activity of Ce&MnCDs to trigger the oxidation of OPD into DAP, thereby outputting an EC concentration-dependent ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric readouts by employing Ce&MnCDs and OPD as the optical signal reporters. Interestingly, these dual-modal optical signals could be transduced into the gray values that was linearly proportional to the residual levels of EC on a smartphone-based portable platform, with a detection limit down to 1.66 μg/mL, qualifying the requirements of analysis of EC residues in real samples. This opened up a new avenue for onsite assessment of the risk of residues of EC, safeguarding environmental health and public safety.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Ethyl carbamate (EC), as a hazardous carcinogen (group 2 A), is widely found in polluted air and fermented foods. It can enter organisms through gas inhalation or the food chain, which poses a potential exposure risk to public health [1–3]. As it turns out, long-term exposure to EC will cause serious harm to organisms (e.g., lung tumors, lymphoma, liver cancer, skin cancer), so the risk of EC residues in the environment has become one of the hot issues in the contaminant analysis industry [4,5]. In this case, governments around the world are highly concerned about the residual pollution hazards of EC and have established maximum allowable residue thresholds for EC in various types of environment and food-related matrices, for example, the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) has established an international standard of not exceeding 20 μg/L for food-related matrices in 2002 [2]. In view of the above, the development of an efficient assays for onsite early warning and traceability analysis of EC contamination is of great significance in avoiding the pollution of the environment and food caused by EC, promoting the healthy development of environment and food-related industries, and thus maintaining environmental health and protecting public safety.

To date, the most effective analytical methods for determination of EC mainly included gas chromatography (GC) [6,7] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [8,9], which are susceptible to the drawbacks of requiring professional technical personals, time-consuming and high cost, failing to satisfy the demand for onsite quantitative analysis of real environment and food-related matrices. By contrast, the fluorometry exhibits the merits such as simple manipulation, low cost, high sensitivity, and real-time virtues [10,11]. In particular, naked eye or portable spectrometers based on fluorescence strategies are well recognized for their ease of operation, fast response and high sensitivity, making them useful for the analysis of EC in real samples [12]. Given such exciting features, several efforts of fluorescence strategies-based onsite assays have been designed to report the residual pollution levels of EC in real environment and food-related samples. As a typical example, Luo et al. developed a ratiometric fluorescence platform based on enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) for the detection of EC by introducing fluorescent silica nanoparticles (SiNPs) into the color-developing substrate OPD system, which has an LOD of 2.6 μg/L, but the reagents required for ELISA were expensive and not suitable for large-scale assays [13]. Another example is that Han et al. used a peptide-based molecularly imprinted polymer (PMIP) to construct a ratiometric fluorescence sensing platform with a detection limit as low as 0.817 μg/L, which is more inexpensive compared to ELISA, but the complexity of the synthesis process of the molecularly imprinted materials and their instability have limited further development [14]. In addition, all of them suffer from a single-mode of analysis and susceptibility to the testing environment. Therefore, it is urgent to have a new method to analyze EC in a fast, convenient, precise, low-cost and reliable way. It has been reported that the fluorescence signals-driven dual-modal onsite portable assay seems to be more capable of providing a built-in cross reference correction for reducing/avoiding the impacts of test environmental matrices [15,16], expecting to obtain a higher accuracy for EC determination, but remains unexplored so far.

In the past decade, artificial nanozymes have aroused great interests to design efficient optical approaches for environmental and food pollutants safety analysis, owing to their stronger enzyme mimic catalytic activity, more robust stability and greater economy than that of natural enzymes [17–19]. Of particular note is that the oxidase-like nanozymes-based optical assays have presented the promising potentials in environmental pollutants safety analysis, because they mainly reply on the target-modulated release/inhibition of superoxide radical (O2 −•) for further inducing the optical signal changes, not involving the poorly stable and toxic H2O2 like in most reported peroxidase-like nanozymes-based assays [20,21]. However, to the best of our knowledge, there is no concern on fluorescence strategy-driven portably intelligent onsite dual-modal assay based on oxidase-like nanozyme for EC detection. Inspired by the aforementioned perspectives, we proposed a novel bimetallic Mn and Ce co-doped oxidase-like fluorescence carbon dots nanozyme (Ce&MnCDs) and further matched with non-fluorescent OPD to develop a portably intelligent onsite dual-modal assay that combined ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric signals for rapid and precise analysis of EC via smartphone readout ( Fig. 1). Owing to the prominent oxidase-like activity of Ce&Mn@CDs to release O2 −•, the non-fluorescent OPD was oxidized into the fluorophore DAP to emitting yellow fluorescence at 550 nm, which was matched with the intrinsic blue fluorescence decrease of Ce&Mn@CDs at 440 nm caused by DAP-launched inner filter effect (IFE), yielding a typical dual-color ratiometric fluorescence readouts. Meanwhile, the naked-eye chromogenic reaction from colorless to dark-yellow was also explore to act as the colorimetric readouts. Nevertheless, the product TCh from AChE to catalyze ATChC could effectively ablate O2 −• to remarkably hampered both the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric readouts, mainly emitting the single blue fluorescence signals and showing a naked-eye colorless. Considering the inhibition of EC’s bromine product (Br-EC, the reaction product from EC in the mixture of Br2 and NaOH) toward AChE activity [22–24], the generation of TCh was also inhibited and the oxidization of OPD into DAP caused by the oxidase-like activity of Ce&Mn@CDs to release O2 −• was re-activated through a competitive effect, thereby restoring the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric dual-modal readouts. With assistance of a smartphone-based intelligent processing system, the transitions of the obtained ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric images into digital color RGB values supported the precisely onsite quantitative analysis of EC residues in real alcoholic beverages, showing the promising potentials of artificial nanozymes-based dual-modal assay for onsite rapid qualitative and quantitative analysis of harmful hazards in real samples, ultimately safeguarding public health.

Citric acid (CA), MnSO4, ethylenediamine (EDA), Ce(NO3)3·6 H2O, o-phenylenediamine (OPD), acetylthiocholine chloride (ATChC), acetylcholinesterase (AChE), 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB), and ethyl carbamate (EC) were purchased from McLean Biochemicals Ltd (Shanghai, China); Urea, lactic acid, ethyl lactate, isoamyl alcohol, arginine, glutamic acid, glycine, MgSO4, ZnCl2, CuSO4 were purchased from Sinopharm Holdings Chemical Reagent Co (Shanghai, China). Throughout the experiment, all solutions were prepared with deionized (DI) water. The purity of citric acid, manganese sulphate and o-phenylenediamine is analytically pure, and the purity of the other materials is chemically pure.

Fluorescence and absorption data were obtained using a HITACHI F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) and a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (PERSEE, Beijing, China); The morphology of the materials was observed using a LaB6 transmission electron microscope and a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Ltd., Japan); Zeta potential and hydrodynamic diameter analysis of the material was performed using a Zetasizer (Nano-ZSE, Malvern, U.K.); The materials were analyzed by X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) using an ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Electron) and by X-Ray Diffraction (XRD) using a Rigaku D/MAX2500VL/PC X-ray diffractometer (Thermos); Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectra were analyzed using a Bruker EMX-10/12 X-band variometer. Raman spectra were conducted on a LabRAM HR800 spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Inc., France).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of oxidase-like fluorescent nanozymes Ce&Mn@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: With reference to previous literature [25], as shown in Fig. 1 A, the described method for the preparation of Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme includes: dissolving 0.05 g citric acid, 0.07 g MnSO4, 0.05 g Ce(NO3)3·6 H2O and 100.0 μL ethylenediamine in 5.0 mL DI water. After ultrasonic reaction for 30.0 min, the mixture was transferred to a reactor, sealed, and placed in an oven for 6.0 h at 180.0 °C. At the end of the reaction, the product was aspirated and 5.0 mL of DI water was added and mixed well. Centrifuge at 12000 rpm for 10.0 min, remove the supernatant and repeat three times. Subsequently, the product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag with molecular weight of 500 – 1000 kDa for 24.0 h in DI water. The purified solution by dialysis was lyophilized in a vacuum freeze dryer, and the lyophilized powder was collected to obtain Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme. For comparison, CDs of undoped Mn and Ce were prepared using the same procedure.

The following six groups were taken and reacted in acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (HAc-NaAc) (pH = 5.0): (1) 50.0 μg/mL of Ce&Mn@CDs + 1.0 mM of TMB, (2) 50.0 μg /mL of CDs, (3) 50.0 μg /mL of CDs (4) 50.0 μg /mL of Ce&Mn@CDs, (5) 1.0 mM of TMB, (6)) 50.0 μg /mL of CDs + 1.0 mM of TMB. After full reaction, the mixture was transferred to a cuvette, and the absorbance at 652 nm was measured by UV spectrophotometer. Steady-state kinetic experiments were also performed by varying the concentration of TMB (0.0 – 1.5 mM). The double inverse plot of TMB concentration versus reaction rate was calculated using the following Michaelis-Menten equation:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted portable visual POC platform for dual-modal visual imaging of EC
ce:section.ce:para: First, a series of 5.0 mL EC solution at different concentrations of 0.0, 10.0, 20.0, 40.0, 80.0, 120.0, 160.0, 200.0, 300.0, and 400.0 μg/L were configured and pretreated with 25.0 μL of the mixture of Br2 + NaOH at the volume ratio of V:V = 5:1 for 15.0 min, and subsequently 20.0 mU/mL of AChE solution, 10.0 mM of ATChC solution, 50.0 μg/mL of Ce&Mn@CDs and 10.0 mM of OPD solution with 20.0 μL different concentrations of treated EC in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0) were fully reacted at 40.0 °C for 20.0 min. At the end of the reaction, it was analyzed fluorescently and calorimetrically.

Subsequently, the fully reacted droplets were taken on a filter paper and the filter paper was irradiated using a 365 nm UV lamp (3.0 W/cm2) at a height of 30.0 cm, thereby obtaining fluorescent images of different concentrations of EC on a smartphone. Similarly, naked eye colorimetric images of different concentrations of EC were obtained without irradiation of the UV lamp. All images were converted to RGB chroma values for analysis using the installed Color Picker APP. In order to assess the selectivity of its assay, relevant interferences such as urea, lactic acid, ethyl lactate, isoamyl alcohol, arginine, glutamic acid, glycine, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+ were utilized as a proof-of-concept for RGB analysis using the same steps.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Onsite POC intelligently analyzes EC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to perform EC assays in real environments, Chinese Baijiu, red wine and cocktails were selected as actual samples, where red wine and cocktails were randomly purchased from a supermarket in Hefei, China, and Chinese Baijiu was randomly taken from a distillery and was not purified. The sample was taken 5.0 mL in a test tube and pretreated with 25.0 μL of the mixture of Br2 + NaOH at the volume ratio of V:V = 5:1 for 15.0 min, followed by the addition of 20.0 mU/mL of AChE solution, 10.0 mM of ATChC solution, 50.0 μg/mL of Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme and 10.0 mM of OPD solution with 20.0 μL of treated sample solution in HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0) at 40.0 °C for 20.0 min in full reaction, then proceeds to fluorescence and colorimetric analyses. The actual samples were also analyzed by GC-MS (Chinese National Standard Method for the Determination of Ethyl Carbamate in Foods GB 5009.223e2014) to assess the correlation between the two methods.

Ce&Mn@CDs were obtained by a simple one-pot hydrothermal technique using MnSO4, Ce(NO3)3, CA and EDA as substrates (Fig. 1 A). The collected Ce&Mn@CDs were then systematically characterized. The XRD pattern (Fig. S2A) showed that the pristine CDs displayed a broad peak near 2θ = 24.6°, which was attributed to the (002) crystal plane of carbon [26]. In contrast, the doping of Ce and Mn weakens the (002) diffraction signal of pristine CDs (Fig. S2B), which was supposedly due to the destruction of the carbon phase by the doping of Ce and Mn [25]. TEM images confirmed that the obtained CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs were spherical with small particle size and well dispersed (Fig. S1 & 2A). The DLS pattern showed that the hydrated particle size of the synthesized Ce&Mn@CDs was about 6 nm, which was significantly larger than that of the CDs undoped with Ce and Mn (3 nm) ( Fig. 2 L). This was probably due to the fact that the CDs were polymerized and the particle size became larger due to the doping of Ce and Mn. EDS spectroscopic (Fig. 2B– G & S3) results indicated that the synthesized Ce&Mn@CDs consisted of the elements C, N, O, Mn, and Ce. Meanwhile, under HR-TEM, the photographed spherical object has obvious lattice stripes with a lattice spacing of 0.34 nm (Fig. S4), which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of carbon, which is in agreement with the previously reported results for CDs [27]. Elemental mapping analysis of Ce&Mn@CD shows that the C element accounts for 85.92 % of the total, while Ce and Mn account for only 1.32 % (Table S1), and these results indicated that the synthesis of Ce&Mn@CD was successful. The FTIR spectra show that CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs are rich in oxygen-containing groups. The band at 3200–3600 cm−1 indicate the presence of O-H and N-H broadening vibrations, and the bands at 2800–2900 cm−1 correspond to C-H broadening vibrations. The band at 2900 cm−1 corresponds to the C-H stretching vibration, the band at 1710 cm−1 is attributed to the CO stretching vibration of the unsaturated carboxylic acid portion of CA and its derivatives, and the band at 1710 cm−1 is attributed to the CO/ CN stretching vibration of amides. The peaks centred at 1384 and 1080 cm−1 should be attributed to C-H and C-O-C stretching vibrations, respectively, which suggests the generation of amide groups by the hydrothermal process and confirms the functionalisation of EDA on the CD surface. In contrast to bare CDs, in Ce&Mn@CDs, the FTIR signals at 700 cm−1 originated from Mn-N/O or Ce-N/O telescoping vibrations, which demonstrated the formation of Mn and Ce active centres coordinated by O- and N-based groups [25,28,29]. The Raman spectra showed G and D peaks of graphitised at 1580 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 (Fig. S6), indicating that the in-plane stretching vibrations of the synthesised Ce&Mn@CD sp2 hybrids and the disordered structure of Ce&Mn@CD [30].

In order to better understand the coordination environments of the Mn and Ce active centres, XPS was used to analyse the metal valence states of Ce&Mn@CDs. The results are shown in Fig. 2H, the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2I) showed the presence of C-C/CC (284.30, 284.73 eV), C-N /C-O (285.45, 286.18 eV) and CN/CO (287.82, 287.87 eV) in the CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs, respectively [31]. The high-resolution N 1 s spectrum (Fig. S7) confirmed the presence of pyridine-N (399.31, 399.17 eV), pyrrole-N (400.66, 401.01 eV) and graphite-N (401.66, 401.50 eV) in CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs, respectively [32]. Fig. S8 showed high-resolution O 1 s spectra with two peaks for CDs located at 532.55 and 532.13 eV, and two peaks for Ce&Mn@CDs located at 530.93 and 530.28 eV, assigned to CO/N = O and C-O moieties, respectively [31]. Notably, we found that the graphite N characteristic peak belonging to the sp2 hybridization orbitals in the N 1 s spectrum showed a significant redshift (400.66 - 401.01 eV) as well as a significant blue shift of the two peaks in the O 1 s spectrum (532.55 - 532.13 eV, 531.10 - 530.28 eV), this phenomenon may be caused by the doping of Ce and Mn, which promotes the electron transfer between these substances and changes the electron density on the surface of Ce&Mn@CDs [25,33]. The high-resolution Mn 2p spectrum indicates the co-existence of Mn2+ (641.62, 653.20 eV) and Mn3+ (643.71, 654.57 eV) in Ce&Mn@CDs, by comparing the peak areas of the two (Mn2+ is 68.13 % and Mn3+ is 31.87 %) indicated that it was mainly Mn2+ (Fig. 2J). Similarly, the four peaks at 898.18, 903.45, 910.06, and 916.06 eV in the high-resolution Ce 3d binding energy spectrum demonstrated that Ce in Ce&Mn@CDs was Ce4+ (Fig. 2K). These results determined the successful doping of Ce and Mn. Furthermore, Zeta potential results showed the average potentials of − 5.24 and − 30.18 mV for CDs and Ce&Mn@CDs, respectively (Fig. S9). The higher negative potential indicated the presence of electron-rich groups (nitrogen-rich and oxygen-rich groups) on the surface of Ce&Mn@CDs, which improved the water solubility and colloidal stability to some extent [25].

The optical properties of Ce&Mn@CDs were subsequently investigated, as shown in Fig. S10A, as with CDs, the two UV excitation peaks of Ce&Mn@CDs are at about 245 and 365 nm, respectively. The former was attributed to the π-π * jump with CC, and the latter to the n-π * jumps with O-H and CO/CN at the material surface [34]. According to Fig. S10B, Ce&Mn@CDs and CDs exhibited very similar fluorescence emission at the same excitation wavelength, suggesting that the fluorescence of Ce&Mn@CDs should originate from the CDs precursor, but the doping of Ce and Mn weakened the fluorescence performance to some extent. Meanwhile, Ce&Mn@CDs exhibited maximum fluorescence emission at 365 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 2M), which was consistent with the absorption position in the UV spectrum. In addition, the effects of solution pH and storage time from 0 - 15 days on the luminescence stability of Ce&Mn@CDs were also investigated, the results were shown in Fig. S11, where the effect of both was negligible, at the same time no change in hydrodynamic diameter at 15 days (Fig. S12), indicating the stability of the Ce&Mn@CDs material, which was consistent with the conclusion of the zeta potential.

From the above analyses, it can be concluded that Ce&Mn@CDs are a class of carbon nanomaterials with dimensions less than 10 nm and have unique optical properties. Next, the formation mechanism of such Ce&Mn@CDs and the luminescence mechanism are specifically explored. Initially, the preparation of CDs was based on the "top-down" method of exfoliation of graphite-based carbon materials, which was relatively complex and did not have outstanding properties [35]. In 2013, Yang et al. achieved a double breakthrough in the quantum efficiency and yield of carbon dots through the "citric acid-ethylenediamine hydrothermal system", which is also the method used for the materials in this study. Specifically, CDs analogues are prepared by a hydrothermal method, a reaction that first condenses citric acid and ethylenediamine to form polymer-like carbon dots, at which point the fluorescent nature is due to the surface/molecular state (possibly due to amide-containing fluorophores); as the reaction progresses from a low to a high temperature, the polymer-like carbon dots are transformed into carbon-sourced dots, which give rise to a synergistic effect due to both the carbon-generating core and the surface/molecular state High quality fluorescence. Carbon-like polymer dots do not have a precise chemical structure, with an internal polymer cross-linking or hybridised lattice structure and an external hydrophilic functional group or polymer chain [36]. When the metals Ce and Mn were introduced, the metal ions were ligand-linked to the O- and N-group functionalities of the CDs and attenuated the fluorescence through the process of electron transfer from the metal valence (Fig. S10B) [25,37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Profiling the oxidase-mimicking activity of Ce&MnCDs
ce:section.ce:para: After synthesizing Ce&Mn@CDs, its oxidase-like activity was then explored by colorimetry based on the chromogenic substrate 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB), whose oxidative product ox-TMB by reactive oxygen species displayed an absorbance at 652 nm and naked-eye blue color. As shown in Fig. 3A,B, the Ce&Mn@CDs + TMB group showed significant absorbance enhancement and colorimetric reaction while other groups did not have this phenomenon. These facts indicated that Ce&Mn@CDs was obtained an enzyme-like activity, which might be originated from the synergistic effect of the doped Mn and Ce as two active sites. The synergistic effect of the two active sites of Mn and Ce was next explored, and the researchers used XPS to measure the content of each element in Ce@CDs, Mn@CDs, and Ce&Mn@CDs (Fig. 2H & S13), and then weighed three nanozymes containing the same mass of CDs and compared their catalytic activities. The results are shown in Fig. S14, where Ce@CDs doped only with Ce did not exhibit oxidase-like activity, and Mn@CDs doped only with Mn exhibited an excellent oxidase activity, which is in line with previous reports [25,37]. However, Ce&Mn@CDs doped with Mn and Ce exhibited stronger oxidase-like activity than Mn@CDs doped with Mn only, which should be the result of the combined effect of Mn and Ce sites. The mixed valence state of Mn(III)/Mn(II) accelerated the transition between two different oxidation states, which participated in the redox process in the enzyme reaction, resulting in the generation of more ROS [25,38–40]. Meanwhile, the valence state of Ce after doping the bimetallic was observed only for Ce(IV), and it was reported that the higher valence state of Ce(IV) had higher oxidase-like activity compared to the lower valence state of Ce(III), resulting in higher catalytic activity [41–43]. But elucidating the so-called “synergistic effects” in bimetallic catalysts is still difficult. Depending on the atomic arrangement of the two metal elements, bimetallic catalysts can be in the form of stoichiometric alloys, intermetallic compounds, segregated structures, or mixtures, which need to be explored further by researchers [44]. To probe the essence of the enzyme-like of Ce&Mn@CDs, the powerful electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements by utilizing 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) as radical scavenger and 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) as the singlet oxygen (1O2) trapping substrate were performed, respectively. In the EPR test, the number and intensity of adduct peaks formed by different radicals captured by DMPO and TEMP may vary. A preliminary judgement can be made based on the number and intensity of peaks. For example, the EPR spectrum of the adduct formed by •OH and O2 −• captured by DMPO consists of four peaks, but the intensity ratio of the four peaks in the former is 1:2:2:1, and that of the four peaks in the latter is 1:1:1:1. The TEMP-captured 1O2 has three peaks with equal heights of 1:1:1 from the left to the right, and sometimes the last peak is slightly lower than the first two peaks due to the blockage of the solution [45]. For this purpose, the researchers mixed 20.0 mL of Ce&Mn@CDs at a concentration of 50.0 mg/mL with HAc-NaAc buffer (pH = 5.0) and added DMPO and TEMP, respectively, and reacted the reaction under light conditions for 20.0 min. As presented in Fig. 3C,D, there were four sharp peaks with the intensity ratio of close to 1:1:1:1 in the EPR spectrum of Ce&Mn@CDs + DMPO (g = 2.00289, AN = AH = 14.85 G, consistent with the characteristics of O2 −•) [46], and no peaks appeared in the EPR spectrum of Ce&Mn@CDs + TEMP, proving O2 −• rather than 1O2 as the reactive oxygen species in the enzyme-like activity of Ce&Mn@CDs. These results suggest that Ce&Mn@CDs have oxidase-like activities, and precursor-derived CDs with abundant electron-donating and electron-absorbing groups are susceptible to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), in which the metal ions undergo a series of electron-transferring chemical reactions with dissolved O2 resulting in the loss of an electron from O2 to form O2 −•, further leading to oxidize colorless TMB into blue ox-TMB [25,47]. By further performing the TMB-based colorimetry in-depth, the Michaelis-Menten constant K m (an indicator showing the affinity of the enzyme to the substrate) of Ce&Mn@CDs was calculated to be 0.34, which was comparable or even smaller than that of the natural horseradish peroxidase (HPR) and other reported oxidase-like artificial nanozymes (Fig. S15 & Table S2). These results demonstrated that Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme had a high oxidase-like activity, whose best work state appeared at pH = 5.0 (Fig. 3E & S16A) and 40.0 °C (Fig. 3F & S16B), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of Ce&Mn@CDs for dual-modal detection of EC
ce:section.ce:para: Having revealed the excellent oxidase-like activity of Ce&Mn@CDs, the feasibility of Ce&Mn@CDs to combine OPD, AChE, and ATChC for developing a dual-modal response system (OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE) toward EC with synergistically ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric readouts was further explored. It has been reported that the mixture of Br2 and NaOH (Br2 + NaOH) could increase the inactivation of EC toward AChE activity, probably due to the fact that Br2 + NaOH solution can structurally derivatize EC by introducing one or two bromines to the amino group of its molecule to produce mono- or dibromo EC, which binds more strongly to AChE than EC, and thus has inhibitory properties against AChE [22–24]. Based on this point, we first investigated the effects of EC before and after treatment with Br2 + NaOH. The results showed that only the EC pretreated with Br2 + NaOH caused a significant absorbance enhancement of the dual-modal response system at 440 nm ( Fig. 4A & S17A). Meanwhile, the mainly blue fluorescence emission of Ce&Mn@CDs at 440 nm was obviously quenched by the IFE process of DAP toward Ce&Mn@CDs (Fig. S26 & Table S4), accompanied by a new yellow fluorescence emission of DAP at 550 nm [48,49], resulting in a remarkable increase of the fluorescence intensity ratio F 550 nm/F 440 nm (Fig. 4B & S17B). On the one hand, these results not only revealed the efficient sensibilization of Br2 + NaOH on the dual-modal response system responding EC. On the other hand, these facts also proved that the excellent oxidase-like activity of Ce&Mn@CDs to catalyse the dissolved O2 into O2 −• for further oxidizing OPD into DAP, which was further evidenced by the inability of OPD to be oxidised under anaerobic conditions (Fig. S18). Then TCh (the hydrolysis product of AChE on ATChC) inhibits it by a competitive reaction with O2 −•, thus reducing DAP production. However, the EC pre-treated by the mixture of Br2 with NaOH made the above competitive effects to be ineffective, because that EC’s bromine product (Br-EC) inactivated AChE to be unable to hydrolyse ATChC, thereby reducing TCh production and facilitating O2 −• work to oxidize OPD into DAP. Regarding these, the effects of the composition ratio of Br2 + NaOH and the pretreated time of Br2 + NaOH to EC on the dual-modal response system toward EC were optimized by measuring its absorbance change at 440 nm. The results illustrated that the maximal absorbance of the dual-modal response system at 440 nm responding to EC appeared at the composition volume ratio of Br2:NaOH = 5:1 (v/v, Fig. S19) and 15.0 min pretreatment time (Fig. S20), respectively. In view of this, the optimal response time of this dual-modal system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE toward the EC pre-treated by the mixture of Br2 with NaOH was also tested at 20.0 min, because the maximum absorbance at 440 nm appeared after 20.0 min incubation (Fig. S21). These findings implied that the analytical time for the entire process for EC detection needed 35.0 min, which was conductive to develop a point-of-care (POC) testing for EC. Apart from these, the other optimal response parameters such as incubation pH, temperature, AChE and ATChC amounts in the dual-modal response system for EC were also tested at pH = 5.0 (Fig. S22), 40.0 ºC (Fig. S23), 10.0 mM ATChC (Fig. S24), and 20.0 mU/mL AChE (Fig. S25), respectively.

Subsequently, we noticed that both the fluorescence intensity ratio F 550 nm/F 440 nm (Fig. 4C,D) and the absorbance at 440 nm (Fig. 4E, F) in the dual-modal response system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE increased with the increase of EC concentration under the optimal conditions. Correspondingly, the yellow fluorescence color of the solution under a 365 nm UV lamp progressively became brighter, and the naked-eye yellow color of the solution gradually deepened as well. In addition, the standard curves both in the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric modes were plotted among the concentration range of 0.0 – 200.0 μg/L, yielding the fluorescence linear equation of F 550 nm/F 440 nm = 0.00644 C + 0.37312 (R 2 = 0.9943) and the colorimetric linear relationship of A = 0.00295 C + 0.37948 (R 2 = 0.9962). All of these results demonstrated the feasibility by exploiting the oxidase-like activity of the fluorescence nanozyme Ce&Mn@CDs to develop a dual-modal response system for rapid and accurate detection of EC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted portably visual POC platform for dual-modal detection of EC
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the results above, we further integrated this response system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE into a smartphone-assisted portable signal readout platform (Fig. S27) by reference to our previous work [50], which employed the digital gray values, i.e., blue (B) green (G) & red (R), from the installed camera imaging and intelligent Color Picker APP inside smartphone as the signal indicator for EC concentration, thereby realizing the onsite accurate POC analysis of EC in the sophisticated and dynamic system. As illustrated in Fig. 5 A, a gradually bright yellow fluorescent images of this response system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE were outputted with the concentration increase ranging from 0.0 – 400.0 μg/L under lighting up the built-in miniature 365 UV lamp, and a series of EC concentration-indicated naked-eye images from light-yellow to dark-yellow among 0.0 – 400.0 μg/L were found as well, revealing an EC concentration-indicated dual-modal greyscale values changes calculated by the built-in Colour Picker APP. By linear fitting the G/B versus EC concentration, as shown in Fig. 5B, a linear expression as G/B = 0.00353 C + 1.09484 (R 2 = 0.9897) among the linear range of 5.53 – 200.0 μg/L was developed for onsite POC fluorescence quantitative analysis of EC with a detection limit of 1.66 μg/L (3σ/S). At the same point, as depicted in Fig. 5C, another linear relationship as G/B = 0.00228 C + 1.07925 (R 2 = 0.9956) among the linear range of 5.76 – 200.0 μg/L was also proposed for onsite POC colorimetric quantitative analysis of EC with a detection limit of 1.73 μg/L (3σ/S). It is worth noting that the detection limit of this proposed dual-modal POC platform not only comparable or even lower than of other reported assays (Table S3), but also lower than that of the maximum allowable residue of 20.0 μg/L the international standard set by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) and the European Commission's target value of 1 mg/L for EC in spirits made from drupes and pomace [2,51]. These results indicated that the proposed dual-modal POC platform had good sensitivity for EC detection.

Considering the complexity in the real matrices of fermented food, the high selectivity and the anti-interference are regarded as the priority to estimate the practical value of dual-modal POC platform for EC detection. From this motive, the selectivity of this dual-modal POC platform for EC over other potential coexistences such as urea, lactic acid, ethyl lactate, isoamyl alcohol, arginine, glutamic acid, glycine, Mg2+, Zn2+, and Cu2+ were respectively explored by comparing the G/B changes both in the POC fluorescence and colorimetric quantitative analysis. As presented in Fig. 5D,E, only EC could cause the obvious increase of G/B values both in fluorescence and colorimetric modes. As displayed in Fig. 5F,H, the anti-interference experiments validated that there were negligible effects of these potential representative coexistences on this dual-modal POC platform for EC detection, because of the nearly consistent G/B values both in the fluorescence and colorimetric modes. Thus, the proposed smartphone-assisted portably visual POC dual-modal platform based on the oxidase-like fluorescence Ce&Mn@CDs had high selectivity and prominent anti-interference ability for onsite precise and reliable detection of EC, which might be suitable for monitoring residual EC in real but complicated environment and food-related matrices.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intelligent onsite POC analysis of EC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the practical application, Chinese Baijiu, red wine and cocktails were selected as test objects. As we know, alcohol is considered as the main content in the wine which might influence the stability of the dual-modal response system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE responding to EC [13]. Based on this point, the effect of alcohol content on the response system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE toward EC was explored by measuring its absorbance at 440 nm. The corresponding result displayed that there was insignificant effect of the alcohol content below 40 %, while above 40 % had a large effect on the dual-modal response system toward EC (Fig. S28), suggesting that the direct testing of EC in the alcoholic beverages with the alcohol content below 40 % did not require any pretreatment, otherwise a simple pretreatment was required such as dilution or evaporative concentration. In this case, as displayed in Table 1, the test results from this proposed portably intelligent smartphone-assisted dual-modal POC testing platform were almost identical with those of GC-MS (Chinese National Standard Method for the Determination of Ethyl Carbamate in Foods GB 5009.223e2014), revealing a high precision and practicability for the onsite POC analysis of EC residues in real Chinese Baijiu, red wine and cocktails, which were further proved by the acceptable recoveries among 97.77 – 105.20 % and the relative standard deviations (R.S.D.) among 1.38 − 4.12 %. According to these results above, it was assured that the proposed portably intelligent smartphone-assisted dual-modal POC testing platform is highly applicable for the rapid and convenient onsite detection of EC, which might offer a promising avenue for avoiding environmental and food contamination by EC residues in a facile, rapid, low-cost, precise, reliable, and intelligent way.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of possible mechanism of AChE activity inhibition by Br2 + EC
ce:section.ce:para: It is well known that carbamate structure has an inhibitory effect on AChE [52]. Generally speaking, the inhibition mechanism of AChE by carbamate structures is mainly the result of the combined effect of the following two: (1) The carbamate structure reacts with AChE to generate carbamoylase, which inhibits the activity of AChE. (2) AChE interacts with carbamate compounds, resulting in the binding site and ester activation site of AChE becoming more stable and thus inactive. In simple terms it is due to a combination of both the production of aminocarbamoylase and the formation of a stable complex [53]. A necessary condition for AChE inhibition by carbamate structures is that they must be compatible or molecularly complementary to the enzyme active site. Thus, only a chemical structure identical to that of the natural substrate acetylcholine will result in inhibition of AChE. And the step at which the carbamate forms a complex with AChE determines the magnitude of its inhibition of AChE activity. Most carbamate structures with significant inhibitory effects on AChE are usually carbamate groups attached to halogen, benzene, and pyridine active groups [54,55], such as the typical anticholinesterase drug neostigmine bromide [56]. The structure of EC, as a type of carbamate, is very similar to carbamate, which creates the conditions for appropriate treatment to match or molecularly complement the active site of AChE and then inhibit its activity. When EC is treated with Br2, the structure of EC is derivatised, introducing one or two bromines to the amino group of the molecule, resulting in ethyl mono- or dibromo carbamate [22–24]. In this design, the apparent changes in the absorbance and discolouration of Br2 before and after incubation with EC indicated that there was a strong reaction to form new compounds (Fig. S29). Through the GC-MS analysis, the characteristic peak of EC located at 89.20. After incubation with Br2, the characteristic peak intensity of EC at 89.20 was remarkably decreased and appeared two new characteristic peaks around 87.20 and 88.22, which was probably to the fact that the EC had lost one or two protons to form mono- or dibromo-substituents due to the reaction of the EC with the Br2 (Fig. S30) [57,58]. These finding might suggest the generation of EC’s bromine product, which was stronger binding to AChE than EC, and thus has significant inhibitory properties against the enzymatic activity of AChE (Fig. S31). At the same time, we also switched to some other common halogen-containing compounds such as NaClO, Ca(ClO)2 and NaBrO, to explore the inhibition effect on AChE by treating the conductivity in the same way. The results are shown in Fig. S32. By comparing the absorbance of the systems, we found that they all inhibited AChE, indicating that they possible can act on EC in a similar way to produce ethyl monochloro/bromo or dichloro/bromo carbamate, which can further inhibit AChE [22]. However, none of their inhibition effects were as good as that of Br2 for EC treatment, which may be due to the fact that Br2 has higher halogen content compared to NaClO, Ca(ClO)2 and NaBrO, so the bromine water can introduce more halogen atoms in a smaller amount to get a higher yield in a shorter time, and Ca(ClO)2 is not so good solubility in water to react adequately. On this basis, an intelligent onsite dual-modal assay based on oxidase-like fluorescence carbon dots-driven competitive effect for EC detection was proposed in this work (Notes S1).

Ethyl carbamate (EC) is an organic compound that is widely found in contaminated air and fermented foods, and is often used as a preservative in life, as well as in various industries such as pharmaceuticals or fuels, etc. If it is exposed for a long period of time or ingested in large quantities, it may increase the burden on the lungs and lead to the incidence of lung tumours, lymphoma, skin cancer and other diseases. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an effective detection method for EC residue contamination in order to establish an effective early warning and management mechanism for EC contamination, to avoid EC residue contamination of the environment and food, and to protect the environment and public health. The Ce&Mn@CDs synthesized in this study have oxidase activity that catalyzes dissolved oxygen in water to O2 −•, triggering the oxidation of the colorless non-fluorescent OPD to the yellow fluorescent DAP at 550 nm and attenuating the blue fluorescence of Ce&Mn@CDs at 440 nm by IFE to produce the typical dual-color ratiometric fluorescence readings. Nevertheless, previous studies have shown that the bromine product of EC significantly inhibits AChE activity. Similarly，our study confirmed that Br2 + NaOH solution can structurally derivatise EC by introducing one or two bromines to the amino group of its molecule, resulting in mono- or dibromo-substituted EC, which therefore binds more strongly to AChE than EC, and therefore significantly inhibits AChE. This produced an EC concentration-enhanced dependent reduction in the visual colour signal and a reduction in the ratiometric fluorescence signal F550 nm/F440 nm, and converted to RGB values for application in portable detection equipment.

To summarize, a novel dual-modal assay based on oxidase-like fluorescence Ce&Mn@CDs nanozyme-driven competitive effect was proposed for EC for the first time, and we applied it to the exploitation of a portably intelligent onsite POC testing platform integrated with a smartphone. Briefly, by employing Ce&Mn@CDs and OPD as the optical signal reporters, the competitive effect between the TCh and EC’s bromine product toward the prominent oxidase-like activity of Ce&Mn@CDs resulted in an EC concentration-dependent increase both of the fluorescence intensity ratio F 550 nm/F 440 nm and naked-eye absorbance in the dual-modal system of OPD + Ce&Mn@CDs + ATChC + AChE, which was integrated into a smartphone to develop a portably intelligent onsite dual-modal POC testing platform through exploiting the images’ gray values to indicate EC residual concentrations. After preforming a series of assessments, the proposed dual-modal POC testing platform exhibited a satisfactory ability for onsite facile, rapid, low-cost, precise, reliable, and intelligent detection of EC residues in real samples. This work exhibited the great prospect of exploiting the oxidase-mimicking artificial nanozyme-driven competition effect for rational design of efficient POC assays toward EC residues, which would also open a new avenue of designing universal platform for various environmental and food hazard factors detection in safeguarding environmental health and public safety.

As a hazardous carcinogen (Group 2 A), ethyl carbamate (EC) residues in environment and food have attracted worldwide attention, but the establishment of efficient monitoring and management system remains a major challenge. To date, the reported portable methods for the detection of EC suffer from a single mode of analysis, susceptibility to the testing environment, expensive reagents required and cumbersome material synthesis (e.g., molecularly imprinted assays and ELISA). To this end, a novel dual-modal EC detection method based on the competitive effect of oxidase-like carbon dots was proposed for onsite warning-early and traceability analysis of EC pollution in safeguarding public health.
